Home → Demo Hub → Objections

FAQ

Twenty stakeholder objections answered plainly.

These answers are written to be readable by executives, technical reviewers, and safety reviewers without hype or ambiguity.

Safety note

None of these answers claim runtime activation has started.

Is this just documentation?
No. It is documentation that is production-verified. The dashboard and validators prove the claims, and the live site serves the current MVP-50 state.
Is this actually live?
The dashboard is live at a public URL. The runtime it documents is not active.
Why are so many features disabled?
Because readiness comes before activation. Disabled capabilities are a safety feature.
Can it execute actions?
No. There are no execution endpoints or active command paths.
Can it write to the database?
No. Public writes and database writes remain disabled.
Can it deploy code?
No. Deploy, merge, and push controls are not in the app.
Can it send alerts?
No. Alert sending is disabled.
Can it roll back production?
No. Rollback execution is disabled. The monitor / rollback / incident console is a readiness surface only.
What makes this safe?
The system carries explicit safety markers, flat validators, and a clear runtime-disabled posture.
What is the next step?
Stakeholder review and feedback. Runtime activation planning is a separate phase if approval is granted later.
Has this been security-reviewed?
Not yet. A security review is recommended before any runtime activation.
Can external reviewers access the dashboard?
Yes. The dashboard is publicly accessible and read-only.
How do I know the dashboard is accurate?
Validators check markers, files, and latest-status content. The outputs are version-controlled.
What does production-verified mean technically?
It means the live site was checked against the repo, the required markers were present, and the validators passed.
What would it take to enable just one capability?
A separate runtime plan, security review, approval gates, monitoring, rollback planning, and explicit stakeholder approval.
Is there a risk of accidental activation?
Not in this build. The app has no runtime activation controls.
Who owns this system?
The repo does not declare a canonical owner registry. Ownership should be tracked in a separate registry if needed.
How long would runtime activation take?
That depends on scope. Runtime activation is a separate planning conversation.
Can I see the source code?
Yes. The repository is public and the docs are version-controlled.
Is this ready for a production operations team?
The readiness architecture is complete. Runtime activation has not started.
